risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Following consent, participants were randomly assigned to condition using a computerized blocked randomization method." Comment: The paper explicitly states that allocation was randomized and that a computerized blocked randomization method was used. Although the exact blocking algorithm is not described, computerized blocked randomization is an acceptable random sequence generation approach when the sequence is generated by a computer.

Summary: The paper states that "Following consent, participants were randomly assigned to condition using a computerized blocked randomization method," but it does not describe any mechanism to conceal the upcoming assignment from the person enrolling participants. Computerized/blocked randomization describes sequence generation, not allocation concealment. Comment: Because no central allocation, sealed opaque sequential envelopes, identical containers, or other concealment method is explicitly reported, foreknowledge of assignment cannot be ruled out.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The trial does not report any blinding of participants or treatment personnel, and the intervention was a clearly distinguishable psychotherapy delivered by therapists in RRFT versus usual clinic care in TAU. Because the main outcomes included self-reported PTSD, depression, substance use, family functioning, and sexual behavior, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence responses and delivery of care. Comment: With no evidence of successful blinding and outcomes that could readily be affected by awareness of assignment, the dominant risk is high.

Summary: The outcome assessment was mainly by patient self-report (e.g., UCLA PTSD Index, CDI, BASC parent/youth self-report, TLFB, FES, and sexual behavior items), with urine drug screens used only to validate self-reported substance use. No blinding of outcome assessors is reported. Because the patients themselves completed most outcomes and they were aware of treatment assignment (no participant blinding described), knowledge of allocation could readily influence these subjective/judgement-dependent measurements. Comment: This creates a high risk of detection bias for the self-reported outcomes.

Summary: The paper does not report exact per-arm dropout numbers in the extracted text, but it indicates that 30 families were randomized and outcomes were modeled with repeated-measures mixed-effects regression across baseline, post-treatment, 3-month, and 6-month follow-up. The article also states that "Treatment completers were defined as having completed 5 of 7 RRFT components. All but two RRFT participants met this criterion," suggesting high completion in the intervention arm, and there is no signal of differential dropout, outcome-related exclusion, or problematic handling of missing data. Comment: Given the small trial and the generally complete follow-up implied by the analysis approach and completion description, attrition is unlikely to have biased the results.
